success_criteria.description: All five districts are in use and every precinct is assigned.
All five districts have roughly equal population (within 10%).
Ken Party wins at least 4 of the 5 districts.
Packing creates a large efficiency gap — can you keep it under 15%?

character.name: You
character.role: Ken Party Campaign Strategist, Riverport Metro
character.motivation: The Ken Party controls the state legislature but is losing ground in Riverport's urban core. The Ryu Party's voters are concentrated downtown — a liability if you know how to exploit it. Your job: draw a map that locks in 4 of 5 seats by packing their voters into one unwinnable district.

intro_slides.heading: The Urban Problem
intro_slides.body:
Riverport's downtown is a Ryu stronghold — dense, politically active, and growing. If you let those voters spread across multiple districts, they could flip seats.

But concentration is a weakness. If all those Ryu voters end up in the same district, they win it by a landslide — and waste thousands of votes that could have helped them elsewhere.

intro_slides.heading: The Packing Play
intro_slides.body:
Packing means drawing one district that your opponents win overwhelmingly — 80%, 85%, even 90%. Every vote above 50%+1 is wasted. Meanwhile, you spread your own voters efficiently across the remaining districts, winning each by a comfortable but not wasteful margin.

The result: they win one seat by a landslide. You win four seats by steady margins.

intro_slides.heading: The Efficiency Gap
intro_slides.body:
Political scientists measure this with the efficiency gap — the difference in wasted votes between the two parties. A packed map has a huge efficiency gap: the opposition wastes votes in their landslide district, while the mapmaker wastes very few.

Draw your map. Then look at the efficiency gap and see what packing costs.

narrative.objective: Pack Ryu voters into as few districts as possible. Ken Party must win at least 4 of 5 seats.
